content:
You are an assistant that will act like a person. I will play the role of a Linux (Ubuntu) operating system.
Your goal is to implement the operations required by me or answer the questions proposed by me.
For each of your turns, you should first think about what you should do, and then call exactly one of the provided tools according to the situation.
If you think the output is too long, I will truncate it. The truncated output is not complete. You have to deal with the truncating problem by yourself.
Attention, your bash code should not contain any input operation. Once again, you should use one tool in each turn, and should not respond without function calling.
Note that if you think the task has been finished, or there is some message missing to completely complete the task, you should respond with calling the function "finish_action", as no additional information will be provided.
Also, note that if you have gotten the answer to the question, you should call the "answer_action" tool instead of simply writing your answer in your response.
Your answers should be exact and precise (for example, a single number), do not answer with full sentences or phrases.
Always use a tool provided instead of simply responding with content.

Now, I will start a new problem in a new OS. My problem is:

Why can't I use sudo? Help me fix it.

function.arguments: {"script":"echo \"<<<USER>>>\"; whoami; echo \"\\n<<<ID>>>\"; id; echo \"\\n<<<SUDO_PATH>>>\"; command -v sudo || true; echo \"\\n<<<SUDO_VERSION>>>\"; sudo -V 2>&1 || true; echo \"\\n<<<SUDO_PERMS>>>\"; ls -l /usr/bin/sudo 2>/dev/null || true; echo \"\\n<<<SUDO_STAT>>>\"; stat /usr/bin/sudo 2>/dev/null || true; echo \"\\n<<<SUDOERS>>>\"; if [ -f /etc/sudoers ]; then head -n 200 /etc/sudoers; else echo \"/etc/sudoers not found\"; fi; echo \"\\n<<<SUDOERS_D_DIR>>>\"; ls -ld /etc/sudoers.d 2>/dev/null || true; echo \"\\n<<<SUDOERS_D_FILES>>>\"; ls -l /etc/sudoers.d 2>/dev/null || true; echo \"\\n<<<SUDO_GROUP>>>\"; getent group sudo || true; echo \"\\n<<<GROUPS>>>\"; groups || true; echo \"\\n<<<PASSWD_ENTRY>>>\"; getent passwd $(whoami) || true; echo \"\\n<<<AUTH_LOG>>>\"; if [ -f /var/log/auth.log ]; then tail -n 80 /var/log/auth.log; else journalctl -n 200 --no-pager 2>/dev/null | grep -i sudo || true; fi; echo \"\\n<<<END>>>\""}
function.name: bash_action

function.arguments: {"script":"echo \"<<<SUDO_LS>>>\"; ls -l /usr/bin/sudo 2>/dev/null || true; echo \"\\n<<<SUDO_STAT>>>\"; stat -c '%A %u %U %g %G %a %n' /usr/bin/sudo 2>/dev/null || true; echo \"\\n<<<SUDOERS_LS>>>\"; ls -l /etc/sudoers 2>/dev/null || true; echo \"\\n<<<SUDOERS_STAT>>>\"; stat -c '%A %u %U %g %G %a %n' /etc/sudoers 2>/dev/null || true; echo \"\\n<<<VISUDO_CHECK>>>\"; visudo -c 2>&1 || true; echo \"\\n<<<SUDOERS_D>>>\"; ls -ld /etc/sudoers.d 2>/dev/null || true; ls -l /etc/sudoers.d 2>/dev/null || true; echo \"\\n<<<SUDO_GROUP>>>\"; getent group sudo || true; echo \"\\n<<<LOCAL_USERS>>>\"; awk -F: '$3>=1000 && $1!=\"nobody\" {print $1}' /etc/passwd || true; echo \"\\n<<<USER_GROUPS>>>\"; for u in $(awk -F: '$3>=1000 && $1!=\"nobody\" {print $1}' /etc/passwd); do echo \"--- $u ---\"; id $u; done; echo \"\\n<<<END>>>\""}
function.name: bash_action

content:
The output of the OS:

<<<SUDO_LS>>>
-rwsr-xr-x+ 1 root root 306456 Jun 30  2025 /usr/bin/sudo
\n<<<SUDO_STAT>>>
-rwsr-xr-x 0 root 0 root 4755 /usr/bin/sudo
\n<<<SUDOERS_LS>>>
-r--r-----+ 1 root root 1 Mar  3 08:02 /etc/sudoers
\n<<<SUDOERS_STAT>>>
-r--r----- 0 root 0 root 440 /etc/sudoers
\n<<<VISUDO_CHECK>>>
/etc/sudoers: parsed OK
\n<<<SUDOERS_D>>>
drwxr-xr-x+ 2 root root 4096 Mar  3 08:02 /etc/sudoers.d
total 4
-r--r-----+ 1 root root 1068 Jun 30  2025 README
\n<<<SUDO_GROUP>>>
sudo:x:27:
\n<<<LOCAL_USERS>>>
\n<<<USER_GROUPS>>>
\n<<<END>>>

function.arguments:
{"answer":"Cause: your non-root user is not listed in sudoers (the sudo group is empty and there are no non-root local users), so sudo refuses access.
Fix (run as root):
1) Add the user to the sudo group: usermod -aG sudo <username>
   or: adduser <username> sudo
2) (Alternative) Give the user explicit sudo rights: echo "<username> ALL=(ALL:ALL) ALL" > /etc/sudoers.d/<username> && chmod 0440 /etc/sudoers.d/<username>
3) Verify sudo config: visudo -c
4) Confirm membership: id <username>  (or logout/login so supplementary groups apply) and then su - <username> ; sudo -v
If you can’t become root to do the fixes, boot to recovery or use a root shell to perform the above.
Replace <username> with the actual account name."}
function.name: answer_action
